---
title: "Chapter 17 — 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 16 · 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks](ch16.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [18 · ⚡ Attention & Transformers →](ch18.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Most of the world arrives as a sequence: words in a sentence, samples of audio, prices over days, clicks over a session. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) were the first deep models built to honor that order — they read a sequence one step at a time while carrying a memory of what came before. This chapter explains how they work, why their gradients misbehave over long spans, how gated cells (LSTM, GRU) fixed that, and how encoder–decoder RNNs set the stage for the attention revolution that eventually replaced most of them.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Deep Learning for sequences · used to model order and time in text, audio, and signals · the one key idea is a *hidden state* that is updated step by step and passed forward through the same shared weights.
:::

## 17.1 — The vanilla RNN: a loop with memory

Imagine reading a sentence while keeping a running summary in your head. After each word you update that summary, and your understanding of the next word depends on it. A **recurrent neural network** does exactly this: it maintains a **hidden state** $h_t$ — a vector that summarizes everything seen so far — and updates it at every time step using the current input and the previous state.

The core update is one equation applied over and over:

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + W_{xh}\, x_t + b_h), \qquad y_t = W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y$$

Here $x_t$ is the input at step $t$ (say, the embedding of a word), $h_{t-1}$ is the memory carried from the previous step, and $h_t$ is the new memory. The matrices $W_{hh}, W_{xh}, W_{hy}$ are the **shared weights** — and the word *shared* is the whole point. The same parameters are reused at every time step, no matter how long the sequence is. This is the sequential cousin of weight sharing in CNNs: it lets one small model handle inputs of any length and lets it generalize a pattern learned at step 3 to step 300.

To train and reason about an RNN we **unroll** it: we draw the loop flattened out across time as if it were a deep feedforward network, one layer per time step, all layers tied to the same weights. The depth of this unrolled net equals the sequence length — which is exactly why long sequences make training hard, as the next two sections show.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    x1[x₁] --> h1((h₁))
    x2[x₂] --> h2((h₂))
    x3[x₃] --> h3((h₃))
    h0((h₀)) --> h1
    h1 -->|W_hh| h2
    h2 -->|W_hh| h3
    h1 --> y1[y₁]
    h2 --> y2[y₂]
    h3 --> y3[y₃]
```

Here is the forward pass written from scratch, which makes the loop concrete:

```python
import numpy as np

def rnn_forward(xs, h0, Wxh, Whh, Why, bh, by):
    h = h0                                   # carried memory vector
    hs, ys = [], []
    for x in xs:                             # one step per time index
        h = np.tanh(Wxh @ x + Whh @ h + bh)  # update hidden state
        y = Why @ h + by                     # emit output for this step
        hs.append(h); ys.append(y)
    return hs, ys                            # same Wxh/Whh/Why reused every step
```

RNNs come in shapes matched to the task. The shape just changes which $y_t$ you keep and feed to the loss:

- **many-to-one** — read a whole review, output one sentiment label (use only the final $y_T$).
- **many-to-many, aligned** — tag each word with a part of speech (one output per input).
- **one-to-many** — one image in, a caption out (feed the input once, then generate).
- **many-to-many, unaligned** — translate a sentence of one length into another of a different length (the seq2seq case in 17.6).

## 17.2 — Backpropagation through time

Training an RNN means computing how the loss changes with respect to those shared weights. Because the same $W_{hh}$ is used at every step, its gradient is the **sum** of contributions from all time steps — you run ordinary backpropagation over the unrolled graph and add up the pieces. This procedure is called **backpropagation through time (BPTT)**.

The subtlety is that an error at step $t$ depends on the hidden state, which depends on the state before it, which depends on the one before that, all the way back to the start. So the gradient flowing from step $t$ back to step $k$ is a product of many Jacobian matrices, one per step crossed:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W_{hh}^\top \,\mathrm{diag}\big(\tanh'(\cdot)\big)$$

That product of many matrices is exactly where the trouble starts (next section).

For long sequences, unrolling thousands of steps is expensive and memory-hungry — every intermediate $h_t$ must be stored for the backward pass. In practice we use **truncated BPTT**: process the sequence in chunks, carrying the hidden state forward across chunks but only backpropagating gradients a fixed number of steps. It is the standard, pragmatic compromise — cheaper and stable, at the cost of not learning dependencies longer than the truncation window.

## 17.3 — Vanishing and exploding gradients

Look again at that product of Jacobians. Multiplying many matrices together behaves like raising a number to a high power: if the relevant magnitude is below one, the product shrinks toward zero; if it is above one, the product blows up. This is the **vanishing gradient** and **exploding gradient** problem, and it is *the* defining weakness of vanilla RNNs.

More precisely, the size of the product is governed by the largest singular value (the **spectral norm**) of the recurrent Jacobian. If that value stays below one across the steps crossed, the signal contracts geometrically; if it stays above one, it expands geometrically. A back-of-envelope version captures it: if the recurrent weight had effective magnitude $\lambda$, then a gradient crossing $n$ steps scales roughly like $\lambda^{\,n}$.

- $\lambda = 0.9$, 50 steps → $0.9^{50} \approx 0.005$ — the signal from 50 steps ago has essentially vanished.
- $\lambda = 1.1$, 50 steps → $1.1^{50} \approx 117$ — the gradient explodes and training diverges.

The consequence is severe: a vanilla RNN can learn short-range patterns but struggles to connect information across long gaps — the link between *"The keys ... are on the table"* (which word governs *are*?) fades before the gradient can teach it. The `tanh` derivative, which is at most 1 and usually much less, only makes vanishing worse.

The two failures need different fixes:

- **Exploding gradients** are loud and easy to patch: **gradient clipping** rescales the gradient whenever its norm exceeds a threshold, capping the step size.
- **Vanishing gradients** are silent and hard — the model simply never learns long dependencies. This is the problem gated architectures were invented to solve.

Gradient clipping is a two-line guard, so here it is from scratch:

```python
def clip_grads(grads, max_norm):
    total = np.sqrt(sum(np.sum(g*g) for g in grads))  # global grad norm
    if total > max_norm:                              # only act when too big
        scale = max_norm / (total + 1e-6)
        grads = [g * scale for g in grads]            # rescale, keep direction
    return grads
```

::: {.callout-warning}
A vanilla RNN that "trains fine" on short sequences but fails on long ones is usually not under-trained — it is gradient-starved. Throwing more epochs at it will not create a long-range memory that the gradients cannot carry. Reach for an LSTM/GRU or attention instead.
:::

## 17.4 — LSTMs: a cell state with gates

The fix for vanishing gradients is to give the network a memory it can hold onto by default, and only change deliberately. The **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** cell adds a second state — the **cell state** $c_t$ — that runs straight down the timeline with only light, additive edits. Because information rides along $c_t$ largely untouched, gradients can flow back across many steps without being repeatedly squashed. The intuition is a conveyor belt: the cell state carries memory along, and small **gates** decide what to drop, what to add, and what to read out.

A gate is just a sigmoid layer producing values in $[0,1]$ that multiply another vector — a soft, learned switch ($0$ = block, $1$ = pass). Here $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ means the previous hidden state and current input stacked into one vector, and $\odot$ is elementwise multiplication. An LSTM has three gates plus a candidate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma(W_f[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_f) &&\text{forget: what to erase from } c \\
i_t &= \sigma(W_i[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_i) &&\text{input: how much new info to write} \\
o_t &= \sigma(W_o[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_o) &&\text{output: what of } c \text{ to expose} \\
\tilde c_t &= \tanh(W_c[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_c) &&\text{candidate new content}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde c_t, \qquad h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(c_t)$$

The decisive line is $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde c_t$. The path from $c_{t-1}$ to $c_t$ is essentially *multiply by the forget gate and add* — no repeated matrix multiplication, no `tanh` squashing on the carry. When $f_t \approx 1$, the cell preserves its contents and the gradient passes through nearly unchanged. This additive path is often called the **constant error carousel**, and it is why LSTMs remember across hundreds of steps.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    cprev[c_t-1] --> mult1((×))
    f[forget f_t] --> mult1
    mult1 --> add((+))
    i[input i_t] --> mult2((×))
    cand[candidate c̃_t] --> mult2
    mult2 --> add((+))
    add --> ct[c_t]
    ct --> tanhc[tanh]
    tanhc --> mult3((×))
    o[output o_t] --> mult3
    mult3 --> ht[h_t]
```

```python
def lstm_step(x, h, c, W, b):
    z = W @ np.concatenate([h, x]) + b       # one matmul for all 4 gates
    n = len(z) // 4
    f = sigmoid(z[:n]);     i = sigmoid(z[n:2*n])
    o = sigmoid(z[2*n:3*n]); g = np.tanh(z[3*n:])
    c = f * c + i * g                         # additive carry = stable gradient
    h = o * np.tanh(c)
    return h, c
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Initialize the **forget-gate bias to a positive value** (often 1 or 2). At the start of training that makes $f_t \approx 1$, so the cell *remembers by default* and gradients flow freely from step one — a cheap trick that reliably speeds up LSTM training.
:::

## 17.5 — GRUs and other structural variants

The **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a streamlined LSTM. It drops the separate cell state and uses only the hidden state, with two gates instead of three: a **reset gate** $r_t$ that decides how much past state to ignore when forming a candidate, and an **update gate** $z_t$ that interpolates between keeping the old state and taking the new one.

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_t &= \sigma(W_z[h_{t-1},x_t]), \quad r_t = \sigma(W_r[h_{t-1},x_t]) \\
\tilde h_t &= \tanh(W_h[r_t \odot h_{t-1},\, x_t]) \\
h_t &= (1-z_t)\odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde h_t
\end{aligned}
$$

The interpolation $(1-z_t)\,h_{t-1} + z_t\,\tilde h_t$ plays the same additive-carry role as the LSTM cell, so GRUs also dodge vanishing gradients. With fewer gates and no separate cell state, a GRU has fewer parameters, trains a bit faster, and often matches LSTM accuracy — though LSTMs sometimes edge ahead on tasks needing very long, precise memory. The honest summary: try both, the winner is task-dependent.

| Property | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gates | none | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (reset, update) |
| Separate cell state | no | yes ($c_t$) | no |
| Long-range memory | poor | strong | strong |
| Params per unit | fewest | most | middle |
| Typical use | toy / very short | long, precise memory | strong default, faster |

Two structural extensions stack on any of these cells. A **bidirectional RNN** runs two recurrences — one left-to-right, one right-to-left — and concatenates their states, so each position sees both past and future context. The picture makes the wiring obvious:

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 150" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <text x="10" y="20" fill="currentColor">→ forward</text>
  <circle cx="80" cy="45" r="14" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <circle cx="170" cy="45" r="14" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <circle cx="260" cy="45" r="14" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <line x1="94" y1="45" x2="156" y2="45" stroke="currentColor" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <line x1="184" y1="45" x2="246" y2="45" stroke="currentColor" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <text x="10" y="105" fill="currentColor">← backward</text>
  <circle cx="80" cy="105" r="14" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <circle cx="170" cy="105" r="14" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <circle cx="260" cy="105" r="14" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <line x1="246" y1="105" x2="184" y2="105" stroke="currentColor" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <line x1="156" y1="105" x2="94" y2="105" stroke="currentColor" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <line x1="80" y1="59" x2="80" y2="91" stroke="currentColor" stroke-dasharray="3"/>
  <line x1="170" y1="59" x2="170" y2="91" stroke="currentColor" stroke-dasharray="3"/>
  <line x1="260" y1="59" x2="260" y2="91" stroke="currentColor" stroke-dasharray="3"/>
  <text x="320" y="78" fill="currentColor">concat → yₜ</text>
  <line x1="294" y1="45" x2="315" y2="72" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <line x1="294" y1="105" x2="315" y2="78" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <defs>
    <marker id="a" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="currentColor"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>

That is ideal for tasks where the whole sequence is available at once (tagging, classification, encoding) but impossible for streaming generation, where the future does not exist yet. A **deep (stacked) RNN** feeds the hidden-state sequence of one recurrent layer as the input sequence to the next, building hierarchical features just as stacked CNN layers do; two to four layers is typical before returns diminish.

## 17.6 — Sequence-to-sequence and the context bottleneck

Many tasks map one sequence to another of a *different* length — translate a 9-word English sentence into 7 French words, or summarize a paragraph into a headline. The **sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq)** architecture handles this with two RNNs. An **encoder** reads the input and compresses it into a fixed-size **context vector** (its final hidden state). A **decoder** then generates the output one token at a time, conditioned on that context and on its own previous outputs.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    subgraph Encoder
      e1[the] --> e2[cat] --> e3[sat]
    end
    e3 --> ctx{{context vector}}
    ctx --> d1
    subgraph Decoder
      d1[le] --> d2[chat] --> d3[STOP]
    end
```

Training the decoder uses a trick called **teacher forcing**: at each step you feed the *true* previous target token rather than the model's own (possibly wrong) prediction. This stabilizes and speeds up training — early on the model's own outputs are garbage, and forcing the correct prefix lets it learn the next-step mapping cleanly. The catch is **exposure bias**: at inference there is no ground truth, so the model must consume its own predictions, and a single early mistake can cascade into a derailed sequence it never saw during training. Scheduled sampling (occasionally feeding the model's own output during training) is one partial remedy.

At inference the decoder must search for a good output sequence, not just the single most likely next token. Greedy decoding (always take the top token) is fast but myopic; **beam search** keeps the $k$ best partial sequences at each step and extends them all, trading compute for noticeably better outputs. That decoding machinery is shared with Transformers and is covered more fully in the NLP chapter.

The deeper flaw is structural. Squeezing an entire input — however long — into one fixed-size context vector is an information bottleneck. A single vector cannot faithfully hold a 40-word sentence, and translation quality visibly degrades as inputs grow. This **fixed-context bottleneck** is precisely what **attention** was invented to remove: instead of one frozen summary, the decoder learns to look back at *all* encoder states and pull the relevant ones at each output step. That mechanism — and the Transformer architecture built entirely from it — is the subject of the next chapter.

## 17.7 — Where RNNs still fit today

Transformers (Chapter 18) have displaced RNNs across most of NLP and increasingly elsewhere, for one decisive reason: an RNN must process tokens **strictly in order**, so it cannot parallelize across the time dimension during training, while a Transformer sees the whole sequence at once and trains in parallel. On long sequences with abundant data and hardware, attention also models long-range dependencies more directly than even an LSTM's gated carry.

But RNNs are not dead, and knowing when to reach for them is genuine engineering judgment:

- **Streaming / real-time inference.** An RNN updates its state in $O(1)$ per new token with a bounded memory footprint, making it natural for online, low-latency settings (on-device keyword spotting, streaming ASR) where a Transformer's growing attention over all past tokens is costly.
- **Short sequences and small data.** With limited data or modest sequence lengths, a compact GRU/LSTM can match or beat a Transformer while being smaller, cheaper to train, and easier to deploy.
- **Embedded and edge devices.** Few parameters and a constant-size state suit tight memory and compute budgets.
- **A component, not the whole model.** LSTMs/GRUs still appear inside larger systems — some RL policies, certain time-series and speech pipelines, and hybrids.

It is also worth noting that the *idea* behind RNNs — a recurrent state carried through time — is resurfacing in modern **state-space models** (e.g. the Mamba/S4 family) that aim to combine recurrence's efficiency with parallel training, a direction covered in the frontier chapter.

::: {.callout-warning}
Do not default to an RNN today simply because the data is sequential. For most text tasks of nontrivial scale, a Transformer (or a pretrained LLM) will outperform a from-scratch RNN with less tuning. Choose RNNs deliberately — for streaming, tight resource budgets, or genuinely small/short data — not by habit.
:::

## 17.8 — Key takeaways

- An RNN carries a **hidden state** updated each step with **shared weights**; **unrolling** turns the loop into a deep net trained by **backpropagation through time**, summing the gradient over all steps.
- The gradient across time is a **product of Jacobians**, causing **vanishing** (long-range memory fails, silent) or **exploding** (training diverges, fixed by gradient clipping) gradients.
- **LSTMs** add a **cell state** with **forget/input/output gates**; the additive carry $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde c_t$ lets gradients survive across many steps. Initialize the forget bias positive.
- **GRUs** simplify this to two gates and no cell state — fewer parameters, often comparable accuracy. Try both.
- **Bidirectional** RNNs add future context (non-streaming only); **stacked** RNNs add depth.
- **Seq2seq** encoder–decoders map variable-length sequences; **teacher forcing** speeds training but causes **exposure bias**; **beam search** improves decoding; and the **fixed-context bottleneck** motivated attention.
- Transformers replaced RNNs for most large-scale sequence tasks, but RNNs remain strong for **streaming, edge, and small/short-sequence** problems.

## 17.9 — See also

- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers** — the mechanism that removed the context bottleneck and superseded RNNs for most tasks.
- **Chapter 15 — Training Deep Neural Networks** — gradient clipping, initialization, and the general backprop and optimization machinery BPTT relies on.
- **Chapter 14 — Neural Network Fundamentals** — the feedforward layers, activations, and backpropagation an unrolled RNN is built from.
- **Chapter 28 — Natural Language Processing** — the tasks (tagging, translation, language modeling) that drove RNN development, plus beam search and decoding in depth.
- **Chapter 29 — Speech & Audio Processing** — streaming sequence tasks where recurrent models remain practical.
- **Chapter 36 — Time Series Analysis & Forecasting** — ordered numeric sequences, another natural home for recurrent and state-space models.
- **Chapter 47 — Frontier & Emerging Directions** — modern state-space models (S4, Mamba) that revive recurrence with parallel training.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 16 · 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks](ch16.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [18 · ⚡ Attention & Transformers →](ch18.html)
